rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE D J H DATED JULY 24, 1990 TRUST
rulings.Rulings: The Motion for Summary Adjudication filed by Respondent Catherine Lewallen and Respondent Ramon Apodaca III (collectively, "Respondents") is denied in part. With respect to the first two causes of action addressed, that is, Petitioners Nicole Biddinger and Debra Duncan's ("Petitioners") claim under Probate Code §850 and related claim for declaratory relief, Respondents have failed to make a prima facie showing that there are no triable issues of material fact as to the §850 claim and thus have failed to shift the burden of production onto Petitioners. Excluding direct evidence does not exclude the possibility that Petitioners could provide circumstantial evidence of the property belonging in trust. Even if Respondents had shifted the burden of production, there are triable issues of fact regarding Decedent's knowledge and intent with respect to the property, namely whether he intended to return title to the trust in light of the facts that he, e.g., deposited the funds obtained through the reverse mortgage process into the trust account and continued to pay expenses related to the property from that fund until his death. See, e.g. Petitioners' Additional Undisputed Material Facts. Thus, Respondents' request for summary adjudication of the first cause of action is denied. Because the second cause of action for declaratory relief simply seeks a declaration from the Court that presumes success on the §850 claim, summary adjudication of that claim is also denied, for the reasons stated above. With respect to the third cause of action for quiet title, a hearing is required. On the one hand, there is no mystery about what Petitioners are pleading, and Respondents have waited until the eve of trial to challenge the pleading. On the other hand, Petitioners have not adequately alleged each statutory requirement, it not clear that this cause of action adds anything to the other causes of actions through which entitlement to the proceeds of the sale of the property can be fully adjudicated, and it is not clear that a quiet title action is appropriate where title has passed through sale to a third party who is not a party to this litigation and whose title, presumably, is not contested by any party. Is a quiet title action limited to cases where the plaintiff claims a present right to title as opposed to claiming a right to the proceeds of a prior sale to a bona fide purchaser based on a right to title at some point in the past? If the claim were to proceed would current title holders need to be joined? Any party wishing to contest the portion of this tentative ruling denying summary adjudication as to causes of action one and two must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, July 11, 2018.
